#Summary1=(human1) Two people discuss cottaging in Muskoka, complaining about the arrival of people from the city without a family history of buliding cottages, who cut trees to build million dollar cottages too close to the shoreline, transforming the wilderness into a suburban landscape while arguing they have a right to do whatever they want with their properties.

#Summary2=(claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929; postedited) Two people discuss how cottaging in Muskoka has changed as wealthy newcomers from cities buy properties and build expensive homes, destroying the natural landscape by cutting trees and altering shorelines, which longtime cottagers resent because it transforms the wilderness they love into something resembling city suburbs.

#Summary3=(gpt4o; postedited) In this conversation, two people discuss the tensions between traditional and new cottagers in Muskoka, highlighting how affluent newcomers who build large, suburban-style properties negatively impact the natural landscape and community traditions cherished by longtime locals who appreciate the area's natural beauty and serenity.

#Summary4=(Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct) Long-time cottagers in Muskoka, Canada, are resisting the influx of wealthy city dwellers buying up land and building large, landscape-altering homes that destroy the area's natural beauty.

#Summary5=(Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) Discussions revolve around the changing nature of cottage living in Muskoka, Ontario, as new, wealthy homeowners build large, city-like structures, altering the natural landscape and conflicting with the traditional, multigenerational, and environmentally conscious approach to cottage life.
#Text=Well , this is the agenda .
#Text=And I am --
#Text=Actually , oh , no , do n't , let 's not .
#Text=Yes .
#Text=No , because they released his salary and I do n't want my salary released because it 'll be so embarrassing .
#Text=Embarrassing you , man .
#Text=Mine is right now .
#Text=You do n't want to know .
#Text=Okay .
#Text=Conditioning has changed tremendously .
#Text=I mean , one of the things some of us , not everybody , fighting is the mega developments and the monster homes going up in lakes .
#Text=And and so these people are coming out to the , quote , cottage or in central Canada or eastern Canada , that cabin or in Quebec , the shed .
#Text=But their vision of what a cottage is or a cabin is totally different from what we were brought up as those who grew up in the environment .
#Text=We may all be boomers .
#Text=Baby boomers and turn of age group .
#Text=We may not be , but we may be .
#Text=But it 's our life experiences that are different , you know .
#Text=1.7 million dollars does n't buy you a whole lot up in the Muskokas anymore .
#Text=It does n't .
#Text=And most cottages were built by the individuals and passed on to the next generation .
#Text=The whole tax structure has changed and the --
#Text=Third generation buys a lot .
#Text=Next door bulldozes it , annexes them both together and builds the 2 million dollar resort .
#Text=No , you 're wrong .
#Text=It 's not the next generation .
#Text=In most cases , it 's people from the cities with a lot of money who have never had a cottage .
#Text=They do n't know what cottaging is .
#Text=It 's -- to them what they 're building is an all inclusive resort , a private , all inclusive resort on a lake .
#Text=And they expect it to either be one totally landscaped like their suburban home , and so they 'll move in .
#Text=They love this area because it 's trees and wilderness , and the first thing they do is cut down the trees , bring in landfill plant sod and make it look just like a city suburb .
#Text=And that is what the true cottagers , the historical cottagers , absolutely resent .

#Text=And , you know , there 's major battles going on in cottage country now because of that with those with the new money who are n't cottagers but are building these major , major things and the old family multigenerational cottages , people who grow up in cottage country who really love the wilderness and want yeah , they want modernization , but they want it on a small scale and they want comfort , but they do n't want it at the expense of blasting rocks , ripping down trees and destroying shorelines and having boat houses across the shorelines every 100 feet that make it look like somebody 's back lane .
#Text=Oh , am I on a soapbox here ?
#Text=Can I suggest that you get off your th- , can I suggest you get off your soapbox for a second ?
#Text=Yeah , absolutely .

#Text=This is because it 's cottaging in Muskoka is I mean , a lot of people think about a cottage in Muskoka as a place to go and a place to get away from the city , to relax on a lake and everything .
#Text=But it it really does have a history to it .
#Text=And it goes back a long , long , long way .
#Text=And it 's not what a lot of people think about cottaging .
#Text=It 's not the 2 million dollar place on the shoreline .
#Text=People .
#Text=People come up here because they like to be on the lake and you sit on the dock and you look around the lake and you see the natural lake .

#Text=And if people come up in the city and they take down all that shoreline and build their million dollar cottages on the shoreline , you wind up sitting on your dock looking around the lake , seeing nothing but million dollar cottages lined up along the shoreline .
#Text=And it starts looking like the suburbs .
#Text=Exactly .
#Text=And that , that was my point .
#Text=And and the debate you get into is , well , it 's my property .
#Text=I can do whatever I want on it .
#Text=And I got , you know , and this is what I want to do .
#Text=And so it 's difficult because , yeah , you do n't want to deny people their rights to do things .
#Text=But the irony is these these newcomers , if you will , move up to this area because of what it has to offer and then they effectively destroy it in order to recreate .
#Text=So instead of building buildings that fit the landscape , they change the landscape to fit their ideal vision of a building .
#Text=And I 'll give you a personal experience with this .
#Text=When I built the place I built here , I wanted to be , you know , close to the water .
#Text=Just I mean , that 's one of the key reasons you 're up here .
#Text=And man , you could n't be closer than 66 feet .
#Text=So I measured out and my corner , my lot , the my building was just almost dead on 66 feet .
#Text=And it -- because of the slant of the building and the shape of the shoreline , it moved away from that .
#Text=That was the closest point .
#Text=People continually come in wanting to build ten , 13 , 15 feet from the shoreline .
#Text=Now zero feet .
#Text=Oh , I mean , it 's incredible .
#Text=But when I was building and I have cottage going back in my blood three generations I too even then , I did n't quite understand it .
#Text=And when I built my place , now I go , " oh my goodness , thank goodness I did n't build any closer . "
#Text=I am too close to the water .
#Text=I should have built further back .
#Text=But we are conditioned from the city to be right on the street and the water is our street and people coming in , they they do n't get it .
#Text=And I can relate because I did n't get it , even with my experience .
